edges.description: BCR engagement activates BTK-mediated survival signaling
BCR signaling facilitates interaction with supportive niches
BTK activates NF-kB-mediated anti-apoptotic gene expression
Anti-apoptotic signaling enables CLL cell accumulation
Progressive accumulation of clonal B cells is the defining lymphoproliferative phenotype of CLL.
Accumulated CLL cells in lymphoid tissues produce lymphadenopathy.
CLL cell accumulation in lymphoid organs includes splenic enlargement.
CLL can produce anemia through autoimmune hemolysis, marrow involvement, or hypersplenism.
intermediate_mechanisms: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia, marrow infiltration, or hypersplenism.

edges.description: CLL can produce thrombocytopenia through immune thrombocytopenia, marrow involvement, or hypersplenism.
intermediate_mechanisms: Immune thrombocytopenia, marrow infiltration, or hypersplenism.

edges.description: CLL-associated immune dysfunction increases susceptibility to recurrent infections.
Active CLL can produce constitutional B symptoms including night sweats.
Active CLL can produce constitutional B symptoms including unintentional weight loss.

nodes.description: Anemia may result from bone marrow infiltration, autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA), or hypersplenism.

CLL cells exhibit constitutive B-cell receptor (BCR) signaling, either through autonomous signaling or antigen-driven activation. This chronic BCR stimulation activates downstream kinases including BTK, PI3K, and SYK, driving proliferation and survival. BCR signaling intensity correlates with clinical aggressiveness.

Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) is essential for BCR signaling and CLL cell survival. BTK activation promotes NF-kB signaling, cell adhesion, and tissue homing. BTK inhibitors disrupt these signals, mobilizing CLL cells from protective tissue microenvironments and inducing apoptosis.

The combination of defective apoptosis, microenvironmental support, and low-level proliferation leads to progressive accumulation of clonal B cells. CLL is primarily a disease of accumulation rather than rapid proliferation.
Enlarged lymph nodes are common, may be generalized, and can become massive. Lymph node involvement is incorporated in staging systems.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical staging of chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

nodes.description: Persistent elevation of blood lymphocyte count (at least 5000/uL clonal B lymphocytes) is required for CLL diagnosis. Many patients are diagnosed incidentally from routine blood tests.

CLL cells depend on interactions with the tissue microenvironment including nurse-like cells, T cells, and stromal cells in lymph nodes and bone marrow. These interactions provide survival signals through chemokines, cytokines, and direct cell contact. Disrupting these interactions is therapeutically important.

NF-kB transcription factors are constitutively activated in CLL and promote expression of anti-apoptotic genes including BCL2, MCL1, and XIAP. This contributes to the apoptosis resistance characteristic of CLL cells.
B symptoms including night sweats indicate more active disease and may warrant treatment initiation.
Increased susceptibility to bacterial and viral infections due to hypogammaglobulinemia and immune dysfunction. Herpes zoster and respiratory infections are common.

evidence.reference_title: The immunodeficiency of chronic lymphocytic leukaemia.
evidence.snippet: Patients with chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL) have progressive immunodeficiency and infection is the commonest cause of death.

nodes.description: Spleen enlargement from CLL infiltration. Massive splenomegaly may cause cytopenias from sequestration.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical staging of chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
evidence.snippet: stage II, lymphocytosis with enlarged spleen or liver or both

nodes.description: Low platelet count from marrow infiltration, immune thrombocytopenia (ITP), or hypersplenism.
Unintentional weight loss is a B symptom indicating active disease.
